brief.alert_summary: RPM alert flagged an elevated home blood pressure reading of 172/106 mmHg with repeat 170/104 mmHg; this is above the supplied urgent thresholds and above the patient’s reported baseline.
brief.patient_snapshot: Patient has active essential hypertension and was prescribed lisinopril 20 mg once daily; clinic BP was 132/82 on 2026-03-14. Patient reported a persistent dry cough and said lisinopril was stopped five days before 2026-05-12; no replacement antihypertensive is documented.

contextual_analysis.text: The alert is a validated Bluetooth cuff reading with repeat elevation (172/106 then 170/104), which supports persistence of the high measurement rather than a single outlier.
The reading is above the alert’s supplied thresholds for systolic and diastolic BP, and higher than the listed baseline of 134/82.
The patient has active essential hypertension in the EHR and was on lisinopril 20 mg daily; a clinic follow-up note documented BP 132/82 with lisinopril continued on 2026-03-14.
Anamnesis indicates lisinopril was stopped five days before 2026-05-12 because of a disruptive dry cough, and a replacement blood pressure medication had not yet been documented.
The EHR/later notes do not provide recent serial blood pressure values or a documented follow-up plan, so short-term trajectory and current management status remain uncertain.

risk_assessment.text: Persistent BP elevation above the alert thresholds may indicate increased near-term risk if the reading reflects the patient’s usual home values rather than a transient spike.
Interrupted antihypertensive therapy could contribute to loss of BP control; this remains a contextual risk consideration rather than a diagnosis.
Normal creatinine and potassium from 2026-03-14 are reassuring for prior renal/electrolyte status, but they do not exclude current risk from the elevated home BP reading.
No chest pain or shortness of breath were reported in anamnesis, which lowers immediate symptom concern in the provided data, but does not rule out clinically significant hypertension.

recommended_actions.action: Clinician review of the alert and home BP context is warranted, with confirmation of current antihypertensive regimen and whether lisinopril discontinuation is still in effect.
recommended_actions.rationale: The RPM reading is repeatedly elevated and the medication history suggests interrupted BP treatment.

recommended_actions.action: Verify whether additional blood pressure readings exist from the patient or device and whether cuff technique/timing could have influenced the value.
recommended_actions.rationale: No BP trend is available in the retrieved record, so interpretation depends on confirming the measurement context.

recommended_actions.action: Review the patient’s report of cough and the lack of a documented replacement antihypertensive in the chart during clinician follow-up.
recommended_actions.rationale: The patient-linked reason for stopping lisinopril may be relevant to management, but treatment decisions require clinician judgment.

recommended_actions.action: Escalate according to local hypertension workflow if repeat home readings remain in this range or if any concerning symptoms are newly reported.
recommended_actions.rationale: The alert remains Urgent and the repeated values are above the supplied thresholds.
